title: REST APIs
description: HTTP methods, status codes, resource design, and best practices for RESTful services.

sections.title: What is REST?
sections.content:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications. A RESTful API uses HTTP requests to perform CRUD operations on resources.

Core principles

  • Stateless — Each request contains all the information needed to process it. The server does not store client state between requests.
  • Resource-based — Everything is a resource identified by a URI (e.g., /api/users/42).
  • Uniform interface — Use standard HTTP methods consistently across all resources.
  • Client-server separation — The client and server evolve independently.

Resources are typically represented as JSON in modern APIs, though XML and other formats are also possible.

sections.title: HTTP Methods
sections.content:
RESTful APIs use standard HTTP methods to express the intended action on a resource:

GET    /api/users       → List all users
GET    /api/users/42    → Get user with ID 42
POST   /api/users       → Create a new user
PUT    /api/users/42    → Replace user 42 entirely
PATCH  /api/users/42    → Partially update user 42
DELETE /api/users/42    → Delete user 42

Safe vs Unsafe

Safe methods do not modify resources: GET, HEAD, OPTIONS.

Unsafe methods can modify resources: POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE.

Idempotent methods

An idempotent method produces the same result regardless of how many times it is called. GET, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent. POST is not — calling it twice may create two resources.

sections.title: Status Codes
sections.content:
HTTP status codes communicate the result of a request. They are grouped into five classes:

2xx — Success

  • 200 OK — Request succeeded (used for GET, PUT, PATCH)
  • 201 Created — A new resource was created (used for POST)
  • 204 No Content — Success with no response body (used for DELETE)

3xx — Redirection

  • 301 Moved Permanently — Resource has a new URI
  • 304 Not Modified — Cached version is still valid

4xx — Client Error

  • 400 Bad Request — Malformed request syntax or invalid data
  • 401 Unauthorized — Authentication required
  • 403 Forbidden — Authenticated but not authorized
  • 404 Not Found — Resource does not exist
  • 409 Conflict — Request conflicts with current state
  • 422 Unprocessable Entity — Validation errors

5xx — Server Error

  • 500 Internal Server Error — Generic server failure
  • 502 Bad Gateway — Upstream server returned invalid response
  • 503 Service Unavailable — Server temporarily overloaded or down

sections.title: Best Practices
sections.content:
Follow these conventions to design clean, predictable APIs:

URL design

  • Use nouns, not verbs: /api/users not /api/getUsers
  • Use plural resource names: /api/users not /api/user
  • Nest related resources: /api/users/42/orders
  • Use query parameters for filtering: /api/users?role=admin&active=true

Pagination

GET /api/users?page=2&limit=20

Response headers:
  X-Total-Count: 145
  Link: </api/users?page=3&limit=20>; rel="next"

Versioning

Common approaches include URL path versioning (/api/v1/users) and header-based versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api.v2+json). URL path versioning is the most widely adopted.

Error responses

Return consistent error objects:

{
  "error": {
    "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
    "message": "Email is required",
    "details": [
      { "field": "email", "issue": "must not be empty" }
    ]
  }
}

questions.text: Which HTTP method is used to create a new resource?
questions.explanation: POST is used to create new resources. The server typically returns 201 Created with the new resource in the response body and a Location header pointing to it.
POST is not idempotent — sending the same POST request twice may create two separate resources. In contrast, PUT and DELETE are idempotent: repeating them produces the same end state.

questions.text: What status code should be returned when a resource is successfully deleted?
questions.explanation: 204 No Content is the standard response for a successful DELETE — the resource was removed and there is no body to return. 200 OK is also acceptable if you return a confirmation body.

questions.text: A 401 Unauthorized response means the user is authenticated but lacks permission.
questions.explanation: Despite its name, 401 Unauthorized actually means unauthenticated — the server doesn't know who you are. For authenticated users who lack permission, the correct code is 403 Forbidden.

questions.text: Which URL design follows RESTful best practices?
questions.explanation: RESTful URLs use plural nouns to represent collections: /api/users. They avoid verbs like "get", "list", or "fetch" because the HTTP method already expresses the action.

questions.text: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?
options: PUT creates resources, PATCH deletes them
PUT replaces the entire resource, PATCH applies partial updates
PUT is for collections, PATCH is for single resources
questions.explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource — you must send all fields. PATCH applies a partial update — you only send the fields that changed. Using PATCH when you only need to change one field avoids sending unnecessary data.

questions.text: REST requires that every request from the client contains all the information needed to process it.
questions.explanation: This is the statelessness principle — one of the core constraints of REST. The server does not store any client session state between requests. Authentication tokens, preferences, and context must be included in every request.
